comment.body: Russia is a country which controls 11% of the worlds surface with 1.8% of its population. They just lost close to 1 million of the productive men and 1.5 million more have left never to return. The gain was around an extra 0.06% of the worlds landmass so they now have 11.06%. When this is over some Russian are going to sit down and think this thru and decide to end Putin

Basically everything the west promised Russia would not happen in the Ukraine happened 
Ukraine DID persecute Russian speaking people etc

He can easily go through history point out all the things Russia publicly objected to a check the boxes showing how the deal reversed much of thst

comment.body: I think not. China is the only reliable regime they can work with. Actually only due to China they were able to survive western sanctions and keep the war going. Its  a longterm alliance.The dictators in both C and R  are for life, unlike western democracies every 4 years new theater new surprises . Putin cant stop war, if he does its a defeat for him, he gained about 50 miles of land in 3 years at exorbitant costs. Imagine Napoleon or Hitler with emperor plans got stuck 50 miles from France/Germany border in some villages🤣. He cant stop, his strategic goals not achieved and he suffered tremendous economic and soldier lives cost. Why would he stop as Trump already showering him with "kindness and gifts". This game of chicken will continue, Trump will be repeating his "peace mantra" and Putin  continue his war blaming Ukraine not wanting "peace-capitulation ".

comment.body: When you have rising gamma you have +ve speed. Speed is the derivative of gamma, so when the slope of gamma is upwards you will have a positive speed, just how +ve will depend on the slopeyness of gamma. Easy enough to work out for a single strike but when you got many strikes it’s a bit tougher I expect. That’s how I understand it

comment.body:
By the way, I tell you a "secret" how can you spare hundreds if not thousands of dollars a year...

If you buy Graham - Intelligent Investor, Natenberg - Options Volatility and Pricing, Bennett - Trading Volatility and Taleb - Dynamic Hedging (very advanced), you spent 185-230 USD only once, (but note that these books are avaible online for free in PDF format)

If you are an IBKR user, real time options + futures data for equities is approximately around $600/year

Lets say you spend $97/month for SG pro, or $224/month for OD promax (including my -10% discount)

It is $3543 in a year, assuming you bought the books and didn't used the discount.

comment.body:
I love the geopolitical discussion! I have given this a lot of thought after Alma presented her infos a few days (is it already weeks?) ago. I think there is whole lot of points to consider. EU and China are both energy poor, so an axis US-Russia-UAE/Saudi could help controlling China and to keep the Europeans in check in the long term. So far China got a third of its oil imports from the two pariah states Russia and Iran and another third from UAE and SA. That’s also why this nuclear deal with Iranis so strategically important for the U.S., if it goes through. But that also means UAE and Saudi will not align too closely with both, since China is a major source of revenue for them. So there is some strategic sense to it. Nevertheless, the simplicity with which it was stated does not live up to the complexities on the ground.  
 
In the short term I think it’s more likely that the US and Russia made an agreement that involved Syria and Ukraine. After the Russians got their asses handed to them in Syria, the resulting destabilization of Syria plays especially in the cards of Israel. The agreement between the US might have been that the US will cease to / drastically reduce their support Ukraine in return of the cessation of Russian activities in Syria. Similar theories were already discussed as a possibility in 2019 for Venezuela or Syria vs. Ukraine. Any long term agreement will require very delicate balancing by Putin. As mentioned, China was besides Iran the only country that continued delivering weapons and technology to Russia inthe early months of the war. It also provided urgently needed income to the Russian state. But as a result of its international isolation Russia ended up being a vassal of China. That certainly did not sit well with Putin, who feels the need to establish dominance and certainly does not like to be the junior partner in a relationship. So the current events are already a major win for Putin. US media is covering Russian talking points about illegitimate NATO-expansion, Putin and Trump are talking about the future of Ukraine and Europe without them being involved, Putin is back on the global stage. Strategically and militarily Russia is still impaired and suffering drastic consequences after 3 years of war. Putin transformed the Russian economy into a war economy running hot. If the war stops, Putin will face a collapsing economy and a lot of social unrest from disgruntled widows and returning veterans. The second is manageable (hello OMON) but in combination both will lead to increased scrutiny of the cost and benefit of the policies of the supreme Vladimir Vladimirovich. Putin thereforehas a major incentive to keep the war machine running. For the peace deal with Ukraine this will mean he will not give in unless it is a total victory (as defined by the maximalist demands from the Russian side) that will present him as the masterstrategist who reinstated the empire. Anything less will most certainly lead to the continuation of the war in Ukraine or the start of a war against some other countries (looking at you Georgia, Kazakhstan) always under the pretence of saving discriminated Russian minorities while serving strategic interest (Georgia is transit country for gas and oil from Kazakhstan and Azerbaijan to Turkey and Greece). For Ukraine, Russias maximalist demands will mean the end of the sovereign state and it will become a Russian puppet state like Belarus. Ukraine/Zelensky of course will not accept this, therefore the war will likely drag on or stay in stalemate. Both sides will pretend to negotiate to make Trump happy, but not much will come out of it.  The Europeans could play a role in this if they were to give up on their strategic paralysis and start acting in their own best interests.
China has an economic interest in Kazakhastan and Georgia, both countries (besides being trade partners and suppliers of raw materials) play crucial roles in the Belt and Road initiative (New Eurasian Land Bridge Corridor,  China – Central Asia – West Asia Corridor). While they certainly play the long game, I don’t think they will sit this out. One imperative will be to increase the cost of this alliance for all parties. Chinas three main levers are threefold: 1. Use their control of the rare earths trade / tech supply chain (chips) to increase pressure on the US and Russia. 2. Use their BRI leverage (in the Caucasus) to increase pressure on Russia 3. In the far east, Russia and China share 2500 miles of sparsely guarded border (on the Russian side). Amassing significant troops along the border will put a strategic dilemma on Russia and the US. There are significant mineral and other natural resources within 500 to 1000miles of the Russian border. Russia will not be able defend its border should China threaten to invade. And besides the US there are no candidates who could come to the rescue. And since Trump promised less wars under his watch, there is a tiny likelihood that will happen. There is of course more nuclear options (China holds around a billion of US Treasuries). Selling them would put massive harm on the plumbing of the US financial system.

comment.body: This is true, and historically, despite both formerly being communist, they were never particularly friendly. I think the west has a cloudy view and tends to think that all communist (or former) countries are allies, but that’s not the case. Just look at the conflicts between China and Vietnam. Also, look at the detente between the U.S. and China during Nixon. Russia just really wants to reassert its sphere of influence over its former states. If you understand Russians, you understand that they have no loyalty to anyone, even their own. Every person takes whatever they can.

comment.body:
Is it possible for you to ‘normalize’ the wording in how you present the data in Substack. I’ve been following you for almost a year on X, I was one of the first 75 subscribers on Substack, so I value your information, I kinda got your gist, or ‘Alma speak’, until yesterday. You used the word ‘centroid’ for the first time ever. Why? The post did not read like your normal posts in tenor or flow. It was unlike you. 

I’m asking if possible, just as you post the reversion levels for Spx below in a list for TV script, can you also list these levels as well, these are from your 3/19 premarket report. And none were included in the 3/21 post.

3/19 Data

> I code 5611.96 as our main pivot level, and 5523.77 (very close to the 5523.5 my momentum model highlighted
> According to the model, as long as the SPX can hold the 5523.5 level, the positive drift remains intact.

3/20 data
> I’m coding 5595 as the level where puts should start to catch a bid in theory for significant downward strenght
>I’m coding 5680.43 as the level where calls should catch a bid;
The main daily pivot is 5623.59, mark it.
>Coding 5683 zero vanna (low vol needed to break through it). Below the 5683 zero vanna line MMs are short vanna, just as I said above. In theory, intraday spot/vol beta should catch a bid below the vanna flip. 
>Coding 5535 zero vanna (high vol needed to break through it).
>5610/5601 is an important support are. If vol can calm down here, price can be stabilized.
>5739.5 is needed to break in order to get gamma support for stabilization.

>5739.5 is needed to break in order to get gamma support for stabilization.

comment.body:
The end to the weekend already but I also wanted to share how last week went for me and some brief lessons learned 

1. The “mean reversion” and supportive themes  of the week as well as the spy flipping green on the daily timeframe at the close of the Friday before had me directionally biased towards calls 

2. Win/loss for the week 6-2. 2 of those wins through the aid of averaging down in lots. I was pretty disciplined about initial position sizing allowing me to have buying power to increase size when I had conviction

3. The week prior we still were trading in the spot decay regime wherein we were taking quick trades and taking profits fast. With this supportive week, I left some profits on the table and missed some larger moves. Eventually I got the hint and by Friday I was allowing price action and especially Vvix/Vix to tell me if we were seeing volatility dropping further when we hit risk levels to allow for holding onto positions longer
